title: Progress Record homes, migrate path, location guard, and viewer history
problem_statement: Progress files pile up flat at the .excn base — thirteen on the dogfood Instance — mixing agent- and gate-written Progress Records with hook-written Runtime Records under one suffix and no naming convention. The most-written record (the session Verdict Ledger) violates the only progress schema that exists. Nothing stops an agent from filing a progress write in the wrong place, already-stamped Instances have no sanctioned way to adopt a better layout (update never touches work-tracking), and the viewer shows only the active sprint and no hook health — past sprints and hook logs are JSON-only.
solution: Adopt ADR-0008: split by writer into two homes — Progress Records in .excn/progress/, Runtime Records in .excn/runtime/ — keeping the _progress.json suffix so the stamped gitignore pattern survives unchanged. Give Verdict Ledgers their own schema beside the unit-of-work tracker schema. Ship a versioned, idempotent, location-only migrate command that doctor points to when it detects the legacy flat layout. Enforce the boundary with a location-only deny guard in the spawn-guard pattern. Extend the viewer with a sprint switcher over the sprint history it already fetches and a hook-health view (doctor-parity cards plus an invocation-log table).

user_stories.want: one obvious, enforced home for every Progress Record I write
user_stories.benefit: my verdicts and step logs land where every reader looks, and a misfiled write is denied with a redirect instead of rotting at the wrong path

user_stories.actor: Instance lead on an already-stamped project
user_stories.want: a single command that relocates my existing progress files into the new homes
user_stories.benefit: I adopt the new layout without hand-moving state or trusting update with work-tracking it promises never to touch

user_stories.want: a schema that matches what I actually append (a Verdict Ledger entry)
user_stories.benefit: my output validates strictly instead of violating a tracker schema shaped for different work

user_stories.want: to browse past sprints in the viewer — lanes, decisions, retro notes, gate verdicts
user_stories.benefit: sprint history is readable at a glance without opening archived JSON

user_stories.want: hook health cards and the invocation log in the viewer
user_stories.benefit: I see a stale or broken hook the moment I look at the status page, with the log right there to diagnose it

implementation_decisions.title: Two homes split by writer (ADR-0008)
implementation_decisions.summary: Agents and gates write Progress Records under .excn/progress/; hooks and machines write Runtime Records under .excn/runtime/. The writer, not the filename, determines the class. The Template ships both directories; the stamp policy classifies them as work-tracking.

implementation_decisions.title: Suffix kept, stems standardized
implementation_decisions.summary: Every record keeps the _progress.json suffix so the stamped gitignore pattern matches unchanged at any depth. Ledger and tracker stems follow <kind>-<id> (sprint-6, prd-008, session, task-<kebab>); Runtime Records keep their feature name.

implementation_decisions.title: Two schemas
implementation_decisions.summary: A new Verdict Ledger schema covers what gates append — the session ledger and a sprint's step_log entries share the entry shape. The existing progress tracker schema keeps the unit-of-work shape. Each kind validates against the schema its kind implies.

implementation_decisions.title: Migrate, never update
implementation_decisions.summary: A new Scaffolder migrate command relocates known records into their homes: versioned, idempotent, location-only, every move reported, content never rewritten. doctor detects the legacy flat layout and names the command. update's never-touch-work-tracking contract is preserved exactly.

implementation_decisions.title: Location-only deny guard
implementation_decisions.summary: A PreToolUse guard denies a progress-suffixed write outside the two homes with a redirect reason naming the right one, following the spawn-guard pattern: rules/decision split, hooks.config toggle, invocation-logged, fail-safe on any error. Content and schema stay the gates' job (ADR-0006 division).

implementation_decisions.title: Viewer sprint switcher
implementation_decisions.summary: The viewer already probes every sprint file; a switcher (active sprint default) renders any closed sprint with the same lanes plus its decisions, retrospective notes, and step_log verdicts.

implementation_decisions.title: Viewer hook health
implementation_decisions.summary: Per-feature health cards mirror doctor (firing/stale/disabled, last heartbeat) over the runtime invocation ledger, with a collapsible recent-invocations table filterable by feature and outcome.

testing_decisions: Migrate command: temp-dir fixture tests — legacy flat layout in, assert every known record lands in its home, re-run is a no-op (idempotent), file content byte-identical; package-qa rubric extended to exercise the migrate contract.

Guard hook: payload-simulation tests in the spawn-guard pattern — misfiled progress write denied with the redirect reason, correct-home write passes untouched, malformed payload or missing config fails safe (exit 0, no output).
Viewer modules: no automated harness; code-standards gate plus manual verification against the live viewer server.
out_of_scope: EXEC-065 (update false-positive drift fix) — already filed and pulled into sprint 6 independently of this PRD.
Schema validation inside the guard hook — the guard checks location only; schema conformance stays with gates and clerk.
Renaming or restructuring committed work-tracking (sprints/, issues/, prds/, retros/) — ADR-0005 layout stands.
notes: Synthesized from the sprint-6 Work Grill held 2026-06-10 in-session (Team Lead waived the fresh-session preference); decisions recorded in ADR-0008 and four CONTEXT.md terms (Progress Record, Verdict Ledger, Runtime Record, Migrate), alignment PASS in sprint-6 step_log.
Release-process observation for the retro: npm 0.3.0 lagged HEAD at the same version number — consider a preflight guard forcing a version bump when template content changes.
